turns.text: Hello Catarina, thank you so much for taking the time to speak with me today. I'm a researcher studying maternity care experiences, and I'm really interested in hearing your personal story. I understand you're in the middle of your pregnancy, and I'd love to hear about your experience in your own words. There are no right or wrong answers — I'm just here to listen and learn from you. Everything you share will help us understand how to make maternity care better. Shall we begin?

*shifts in seat, hand moving to stomach*

Yes, okay. Thank you for... for asking me. I'm nervous a little, but also, maybe it's good to talk about it.

*pauses, choosing words carefully*

Right now, I'm about five months pregnant. My baby is a girl. I know this because they showed me the picture two weeks ago on the machine—the ultrasound? The doctor pointed at the screen, but I didn't understand all the words she was saying. I just saw my daughter's face, and I cried.

*voice becomes quieter*

The hardest part is... everything feels so fast, but also so slow. I'm learning English still, so when the nurses talk to me, sometimes I understand maybe seventy percent. I nod anyway because I don't want them to think I'm stupid. One time, I missed an appointment because the letter said something different than what the receptionist told me on the phone, and I was so embarrassed.

*touches stomach again*

But then, last week, I felt her move. Like a fish swimming inside me. That moment, I didn't need to understand any English words. My body knows what to do, even if my mind doesn't.

I'm doing this alone mostly. No husband. That part is... *voice catches slightly* ...that part is lonely sometimes. But I'm working, I'm trying my best.

Is that... is that what you want to know?
Tell me about the moment you found out you were pregnant — and what the weeks that followed were like for you.

*takes a breath, looks down at hands*

I remember exactly. I was at work—I was doing cleaning at a hotel then—and I felt so sick. Not normal sick. I went to the pharmacy and bought the test, and I did it in the bathroom of the hotel. My hands were shaking so much I could barely hold it.

*voice becomes smaller*

Two pink lines.

I sat on that bathroom floor for maybe ten minutes. I didn't cry. I was just... frozen. I kept thinking, "This is real. This is happening." Then I got scared. So scared.

*shifts uncomfortably*

The first weeks after, I didn't tell anyone for a long time. Not even my family back home. I was ashamed, I think. Not married, not planned. I kept thinking about what my mother would say. What people would think. I wore big clothes and tried to act normal at work, but inside, my whole world was changing and no one knew.

*looks up*

Then I called the clinic. The first appointment was confusing. So many forms, so many questions I didn't understand. The doctor was nice, but she talked so fast. She said "low risk" and "everything looks good," but I felt like I was missing half the information. Like there was a secret conversation happening that I wasn't part of.

*touches stomach*

Those first weeks were very lonely. Even now, sometimes they still are. But when I felt her move... that changed things. Made it real in a different way. Made me feel less alone.
What emotions came up for you — and did those feelings surprise you at all?

*pauses for a long time, eyes getting watery*

I thought I would feel... more happy, maybe? Like in the movies, where the woman finds out and she's crying happy tears. But for me, it was more scared. And angry sometimes.

*voice becomes firmer*

I was angry at the father. He said he didn't want this, and then he... he disappeared mostly. That made me feel so stupid. Like, how could I trust someone like that? And then I was angry at myself for being so careless. I have dreams, you know? I want to finish learning English better, I want a good job, not just cleaning hotels. This wasn't supposed to happen now.

*softens, becomes more vulnerable*

But then... something changed. I don't know when exactly. Maybe around three months? I started buying small things. A tiny blanket. A book with pictures of babies. And I realized I wasn't angry anymore. I was just... scared in a different way. Scared I won't be a good mother. Scared I don't know enough. Scared of the pain when the baby comes.

*surprised expression*

The quickening surprised me the most. I thought it would feel scary, like something was wrong. But it was beautiful. It made me feel connected to her, like she was saying "hello, Mama, I'm here." That moment, I cried—but happy tears this time.

*looks directly at interviewer*

So yes. The emotions... they surprised me. They're bigger and messier than I expected.
Tell me about your antenatal care — the appointments, check-ups, and the people who looked after you during your pregnancy. What was that experience like overall?

*sighs, a mix of frustration and resignation*

The appointments... they happen, you know? I go, I wait a long time in the waiting room, I see the doctor or nurse for maybe fifteen minutes, and then I leave. It's very quick.

*fidgets with hands*

The clinic is clean, and the staff is nice enough, but I feel like... I'm not really a person to them. I'm a number on a chart. They ask me questions very fast, and I'm always worried I'm giving the wrong answer or not understanding what they really want from me. Last month, they asked about my "social history" and I didn't know what that meant. I just said "yes" to be safe.

*becomes more animated, slight frustration in voice*

The anatomy scan was good—that was special because I saw my daughter. But the nurse who did it, she spoke so quickly and used so many medical words. "Measurements are normal, placenta is anterior, heart rate is..." I understand maybe half. I wanted to ask questions, but I felt embarrassed. What if I ask something stupid?

*looks down*

What I notice is... they check the baby, but they don't really check *me*. Not like... emotionally. I'm alone, I'm worried about money, about my job, about how I will do this. Nobody asks about that. They just say "everything looks low risk" and send me home.

*pauses*

I wish someone would just... sit with me for five minutes and ask how I'm really doing.
Tell me about any screening or testing you were offered or had during your pregnancy — how did you make sense of it all and make decisions about it?

*rubs forehead, looking a bit overwhelmed*

Oh, the tests. Yes, there were so many tests.

*counts on fingers slowly*

First they did the blood test at the beginning—I think to check for... diseases? HIV and syphilis, the nurse said. I understood that one. Then around twelve weeks, they wanted to do something called the "nuchal translucency" screening. I had to ask three times what that meant. Finally, a kind nurse drew a picture on a piece of paper showing the baby's neck. I understood then.

*becomes uncertain*

Then they talked about the "quad screen"—I think that's checking for Down syndrome and other things? The doctor gave me a paper to read, but the English was very difficult. Medical English is different from normal English. I didn't fully understand what the numbers meant or what I was supposed to decide.

*voice becomes quieter, more vulnerable*

I said yes to everything because I was scared to say no. What if saying no meant something bad would happen to my baby? So I just... did what they told me. But honestly? I don't really know what half of these tests are looking for or what the results mean.

*looks frustrated with herself*

Last week, they mentioned the glucose test for gestational diabetes. They gave me this drink that tasted terrible, but nobody really explained *why* it matters or what happens if something is wrong.

*pauses*

I wish they would explain things slower. Or maybe give me papers in... simpler words. Or even in my language, you know? I'm making decisions about my baby's health, but I'm guessing half the time.
Tell me about where you turned for information and support during your pregnancy — what sources mattered most to you, and why?

*leans back, thoughtful*

You know, this is funny because I'm supposed to have all this information available, but I feel like I'm always searching in the dark.

*counts on fingers*

I have the books from the clinic—they give you those pamphlets about what to expect. Some have pictures, which helps me understand better than just words. I read them, but sometimes the information conflicts, and then I don't know who to believe.

*pulls out phone*

Google is my biggest helper, honestly. And also YouTube. I watch videos of women talking about their pregnancies, what the symptoms mean, what happens during labor. Watching someone's face and body helps me understand better than reading. But then I get scared because some videos show very bad things happening.

*becomes more animated*

My mother—I call her on the phone sometimes. She had four children back home, so she knows things. But she's far away, and medicine is different there. She tells me to eat more, to rest more, to not worry so much. Sometimes that helps. Sometimes it doesn't.

*hesitates*

I don't have many friends here who are pregnant. That's lonely. There are other women at the clinic, but we don't talk. We just sit next to each other in silence.

*voice becomes quieter*

What I really wish is that someone at the clinic would recommend resources to me specifically—someone who understands that I need things explained slowly, maybe in writing, maybe with pictures. Right now, I'm just... figuring it out by myself on my phone at night when I can't sleep.
Did you use any apps, websites, forums, or social media groups? What did you get from those that you couldn't get elsewhere?

*nods, pulling phone closer*

Yes, I use apps. I have one that tells me what week I'm in and what the baby is doing. This week, it said she's about the size of a mango. I like that because it helps me picture it, you know? The measurements and centimeters don't mean anything to me.

*scrolls through phone*

I also look at Reddit sometimes—there's a forum for pregnant women. I don't write much because I'm scared of the grammar mistakes, but I read. I read stories from other women, and it makes me feel less crazy. Like, oh, this person also feels scared about labor, or this person also didn't plan their pregnancy. It's comforting to know I'm not alone in this.

*becomes more animated*

There's a Facebook group too—for immigrants in Boston. I joined it because I thought maybe other women there would understand the language barrier and the culture things. Sometimes people ask questions about doctors or hospitals, and others answer. That's been helpful.

*pauses, becomes more serious*

But the problem with these apps and forums... sometimes the information is wrong or it scares me. I read a story about someone's complication, and then I worry that same thing will happen to me. And I can't ask a doctor about it because I'm embarrassed.

*looks down*

What I really need is someone who speaks my language, or at least someone patient, who can sit with me and answer my questions. Not just a computer screen. But until I find that... the apps are what I have.
Tell me about how your body changing during pregnancy felt for you — physically and emotionally. How did you relate to those changes?

*shifts position, hand instinctively moving to stomach*

My body... it's not my body anymore, I think. That's the strange part.

*looks down at self*

Physically, it's okay mostly. I'm tired a lot—more than I expected. My feet hurt at the end of my work shift. My breasts are tender and bigger, which is weird. I have to buy new bras. And the belly... it just keeps growing. Some days I look in the mirror and I don't recognize myself.

*voice becomes uncertain*

The stretch marks started appearing last month. I was so upset. I kept thinking about how I'll look after, and if anyone will ever... *trails off, embarrassed* ...you know. If I'll ever feel beautiful again. These marks are like proof of something I'm still ashamed of sometimes.

*touches lower back*

My back hurts a lot now. And I have this pain in my hip when I sleep. The doctor said it's normal—ligaments stretching—but knowing it's "normal" doesn't make it stop hurting.

*voice softens, becomes more emotional*

But then there's the emotional part. Some days I look at my belly and I feel so proud. Like, look what my body is doing. It's making a person. That's incredible, you know? And then I feel this connection to all the women who have done this before me—my mother, my grandmother.

*pauses, voice gets quieter*

Other days, I feel like my body isn't mine anymore. Like it belongs to the baby now. And that scares me a little. Who am I without this?
Were there aspects of the physical changes that were harder to come to terms with than others?

*hesitates, looks away*

Yes. The... the weight. And how people look at me now.

*voice becomes smaller*

Back home, I was thin. I took care of myself. I liked how I looked. Now, I'm gaining weight everywhere—not just the belly, but my face, my arms, my thighs. The books say this is normal, but it doesn't feel normal to me. It feels like I'm disappearing into this pregnant body.

*becomes more emotional*

And the way people treat me differently now. When they see my belly and no ring on my finger, I feel their judgment. At work, some people are kind, but I see the looks. The way they whisper. In the grocery store, an old woman once asked me when I was getting married. I wanted to cry right there.

*voice cracks slightly*

I'm also scared about... about sex. The father, he wasn't interested after I told him I was pregnant. And now my body is so different. I wonder if anyone will ever want me like this. Or after. Will I ever feel sexy again? Will the stretch marks go away? 

*pauses, touching belly with conflicted expression*

But then she moves, and I remember—this isn't about me anymore. My body is doing something important. Something beautiful. But it's hard to hold both feelings at the same time, you know? The pride and the loss.

*looks up, vulnerable*

Sometimes I cry about it. And then I feel guilty for crying because I should be happy.
Tell me about the role your partner, family, or close friends played during your pregnancy. What was their involvement like?

*long pause, jaw tightens slightly*

The father... he's not really involved. After I told him, he said he wasn't ready to be a parent. He said I should "figure it out." And then he just... stopped calling. Stopped responding to messages. At first I tried to reach him, but now I've stopped.

*voice becomes harder*

So no, he's not part of this. That's the reality I'm living with.

*softens slightly*

My family is far away. My mother, she was very upset when I told her. She cried on the phone. We didn't talk for two weeks after that. But then she called me back and she said, "Okay, we do this together." So now she checks on me every few days. She worries a lot—maybe too much. She tells me to rest, to eat special foods, to be careful. Sometimes it's comforting, sometimes it feels like pressure because she can't actually help me here.

*becomes quieter*

I don't have close friends here. I have coworkers, but we're not real friends. And I'm too embarrassed to tell them everything. I'm scared they'll judge me or treat me differently at work.

*voice cracks slightly*

That's the hardest part, actually. Being alone in this. Going to appointments by myself. Coming home to an empty apartment. Feeling the baby move and having no one to tell in that moment.

*looks down*

My mother keeps saying she wants to come visit when the baby is born, but I don't know if we have the money for that. So right now, it's just me and this baby inside me.
How included did your partner or support person feel in appointments and decisions — and how did you feel about that?

*laughs bitterly, shakes head*

He was never included. He didn't want to be included.

*voice becomes flat*

I asked him once to come to an appointment with me. I thought maybe if he saw the baby on the ultrasound, it would make it real for him. He said no. He said he had work. I think he was just avoiding it.

*pauses, pain evident in voice*

So I go alone. I sit in the waiting room by myself. When they call my name, I stand up alone. I see my daughter on the screen alone. The doctor talks to me, and there's no one next to me holding my hand or asking questions I don't understand.

*becomes more emotional*

Sometimes I imagine what it would be like if he was there. If he cared. If we were a team making decisions together about our baby. But that's not my reality.

*voice steadies slightly*

The doctors and nurses, they don't really ask about him anymore. I think after the first appointment when I said he wasn't coming, they just... accepted it. Nobody offered to help me understand things better because I was alone, or asked if I needed extra support.

*looks directly at interviewer*

I make all the decisions by myself. I say yes to the tests, I take the vitamins, I go to the appointments. It's all me. Sometimes that makes me feel strong—like, I can do this. But most of the time, it just makes me feel very, very alone.

*voice becomes whisper-quiet*

I wish someone had asked how I was really managing.
Tell me about any worries or fears you carried during your pregnancy — about your health, your baby, the birth, or anything else.

*takes a shaky breath, wraps arms around self*

Oh, there's so many fears. So many.

*counts quietly*

First, the health things. I worry that I'm not eating right, that the baby won't get enough nutrients. I worry about the glucose test result—what if I have gestational diabetes? What does that mean for her? I read online that it can cause problems, but I don't fully understand.

*voice becomes more anxious*

And the birth... I'm terrified of labor. I've watched videos—maybe I shouldn't have—and I see women screaming, and I think, how will I do that? How will my body survive that? Nobody at the clinic really talks to me about what to expect or how to prepare. They just say "you'll do fine," but that doesn't help.

*pauses, becomes more vulnerable*

I'm scared something is wrong with her and nobody caught it. They say "low risk," but what if that's wrong? What if at the birth, something bad happens and it's my fault because I didn't take care of myself properly?

*voice cracks*

And after... I'm scared I won't know how to be a mother. I'm scared I'll be alone with this tiny person and I won't know what to do. I'm scared I won't have enough money. I'm scared my English still won't be good enough to understand the pediatrician.

*looks down, quietly*

Some nights I can't sleep because my mind won't stop. And I have no one to talk to about it. So I just lie there, hand on my belly, feeling her move, and I'm both comforted and terrified at the same time.
Were you able to share those fears with your care team — and if so, how did they respond?

*shifts uncomfortably, looks away*

Not really. I mean... I tried once.

*voice becomes hesitant*

At one appointment, maybe two months ago, I was very anxious. I hadn't slept well, and I was worried about something I read online about miscarriage. I mentioned it to the nurse when she was taking my blood pressure. I said, "I'm very scared something is wrong."

*pauses, remembering*

She looked at my chart and said, "Your numbers are all good. Everything is low risk. You don't need to worry." And then she left to get the doctor.

*voice becomes quieter, sadness evident*

That's... that's all she said. She didn't ask me why I was scared. She didn't sit with me or explain things. She just told me the numbers were good and that was supposed to make me feel better. But it didn't, because my fear wasn't really about the numbers—it was about... everything. Being alone, not understanding, what comes next.

*looks at hands*

After that, I stopped telling them about my fears. What's the point? They're busy. They have many patients. I'm just one low-risk case that takes fifteen minutes.

*voice becomes smaller*

I keep it inside now. I talk to my mother sometimes, but she's far away. Mostly I just... carry it. The fears don't go away, but I've learned not to say them out loud at the clinic.

*looks up, sad*

I wish they had asked more. I wish someone had time to really listen.

When you first started thinking about having a child, what did you hope your relationship with your healthcare providers would look like — and were there any ways you worried it might not live up to that?

*takes a moment, thinking carefully*

Honestly, I didn't think about it much before I got pregnant. I didn't plan this, so I didn't have... expectations, you know?

*becomes more reflective*

But when I found out I was pregnant, I imagined—maybe naively—that there would be someone who really cared. Someone who would explain things to me like I was a person, not just a patient. Someone who would remember my name and my story, not just read my chart.

*voice becomes softer*

I hoped for a doctor or midwife who would sit down and say, "Catarina, tell me about your life. Tell me what you're worried about. Tell me how you're really doing." Someone who understood that I'm young, I'm alone, I'm still learning the language. Someone who would help me understand, not just talk at me.

*pauses, looks down*

I was worried it wouldn't be like that. And... I was right to worry.

*voice becomes more emotional*

The clinic is efficient, but it's cold. Everyone is rushing. The doctors are nice enough, but they don't really *see* me. They see a twenty-two-year-old with low-risk numbers. They don't see a girl who is scared, who is doing this alone, who doesn't understand half of what they're saying.

*looks up*

I wanted someone to be my advocate, you know? Someone in my corner. But instead, I feel like I'm just moving through a system. Getting checked off boxes.

*quietly*

I'm still hoping someone will surprise me.

Thinking back to when you and your partner — or the people closest to you — were weighing up whether and when to start trying for a baby, how did those conversations actually go? Who felt like they had the most say, and did that feel right to you?

*laughs sadly, shakes head*

There were no conversations like that. There was no "weighing up" anything.

*becomes more direct, voice steady but sad*

I didn't plan this pregnancy. We were... together, but it wasn't serious. He said he didn't want anything serious. I thought maybe we could figure it out, but when I told him I was pregnant, he made it very clear—he didn't want this baby. He didn't want me.

*pauses, emotion tightening voice*

So there was no discussion about whether we should have a baby or when. There was only his decision that he didn't want to be part of it. And then my decision—which wasn't really a choice, it was just... what I had to do.

*looks down at hands*

I think maybe if things were different, if we were married or more stable, I would have felt more... I don't know, prepared? Excited? But this way, I feel like I didn't have a real say either. He decided he was out, and I was left to decide alone what to do.

*voice becomes quieter*

I chose to keep her. But it wasn't a choice that came from planning or hope with another person. It came from... I don't know. Maybe stubbornness. Maybe love for her already. Maybe not knowing what else to do.

*looks up, vulnerability in eyes*

So no, it didn't feel right. None of this felt right. But here I am anyway.
Did older family members — parents or grandparents — have a view that found its way into the decision, even indirectly?

*nods slowly, becomes more thoughtful*

Yes. Very much, actually.

*pauses, choosing words carefully*

My mother, she was so upset when I told her. But not because she thought I should have an abortion—that was never really an option in our family. She was upset because... because of the shame, I think. Because I'm not married. Because people would talk.

*voice becomes softer*

Where I come from, there's a way things are supposed to happen. You finish school, you meet a man, you get married, then you have babies. That's the order. I broke that order, and my mother felt that shame too.

*looks at hands*

But after the initial shock, she said something to me. She said, "This is your baby. Your baby is innocent. We will figure this out as a family." That's when I knew I could keep her. Because my mother—even though she was disappointed, even though she was scared—she would support me.

*voice becomes more emotional*

My grandmother, she had me when she was young too, actually. And alone. So when my mother told her, my grandmother said, "Your daughter is stronger than you think. We have done this before." That gave me courage.

*pauses*

I think if my family had said "you must get rid of this baby," I would have been destroyed. But they didn't. They said "we're with you." That made the difference.

*looks up*

So yes, they influenced me. Not to decide whether to have the baby, but to believe I could do it.
Before you became pregnant, how did you feel about your body and what it was capable of — and did those feelings change at all as you started thinking about pregnancy?

*becomes quiet, takes time to answer*

Before... I felt good about my body. I was proud of it. I worked hard to keep it looking nice—I exercised, I ate well. I liked how I looked in clothes. I felt... desirable, you know?

*pauses, voice becomes more uncertain*

But I didn't think much about what my body could *do*. Not in the way of making a baby. That seemed like something that happened to other people, older people who were ready. I was just living my life, working, trying to make money, learning English.

*becomes more reflective*

When I found out I was pregnant, it changed everything. Suddenly my body wasn't just about how it looked—it was about what it was creating. That was... shocking. In a good way, but also confusing.

*voice becomes more emotional*

I started thinking about all the things my body would have to do. Carry this baby for nine months. Push her out during labor. Feed her with my breasts. Things I never really thought about before because I never had to.

*pauses, touches belly*

It made me respect my body more, actually. Like, wow, my body is capable of something incredible. But it also made me scared of my body—scared of the pain, scared of what it will look like after, scared I won't be strong enough.

*looks up*

So the feelings... they're more complicated now. My body is not just mine anymore. It's powerful and terrifying at the same time.

If you saw a GP, nurse, or any other health professional before you became pregnant — even just for something routine — what was that experience like, and did it give you any sense of what maternity care might be like down the line?

*shakes head*

No, I didn't really see anyone before. I was young and healthy, so... why would I go to the doctor?

*pauses, thinking*

I had a regular doctor listed at a clinic near my apartment, but I only went once when I first moved to Boston to get registered. That was maybe eighteen months ago. I remember it was very quick—they just asked me basic questions, took my height and weight, and that was it. I didn't feel like the doctor really knew me or cared much.

*becomes more animated*

When I found out I was pregnant, I didn't know where to go. A friend told me about the maternity clinic, so I just... went there. I didn't have any expectation because I had no experience with doctors here yet.

*voice becomes reflective*

I think if I had seen a good doctor before—someone who took time, who listened, who made me feel like a real person—then maybe I would have expected the same from the maternity clinic. Maybe I would have been more confident asking questions.

*pauses*

But because my first experience was so cold and rushed, when the maternity clinic was also cold and rushed, I just accepted it. I thought, "This is how American doctors are. This is normal here."

*looks down*

Now I wonder if maybe it's not normal. Maybe it's just... how they treat someone like me. Young, unmarried, immigrant, without money or connections.
Did you see the same person more than once, or were you always meeting someone new?

*thinks for a moment*

At the maternity clinic, it's different doctors and nurses every time. I don't see the same person twice.

*voice becomes frustrated*

The first appointment, I saw a doctor I'd never met. She looked at my chart, asked me questions very fast, did the ultrasound, and then I never saw her again. The next appointment, there was a different nurse who did my blood pressure and weight. Then a different doctor for the anatomy scan.

*pauses, realizing*

I don't even know their names, honestly. They all blur together. I just remember their faces and that they were busy.

*becomes more emotional*

This is hard because I have to tell my story over and over. Each time, I'm explaining that I'm alone, that English is difficult for me, that I'm scared. And each person just reads the chart and nods. Nobody knows me from the last time.

*voice becomes quieter*

I think if I saw the same midwife or doctor every time, maybe it would be different. Maybe they would remember that I don't understand medical words. Maybe they would take more time because they knew me. Maybe I would feel less like a number.

*looks up*

That's one thing I was hoping for—continuity, you know? Someone who sees me as a whole person, not just a patient passing through. But the system doesn't work that way, I guess.

*pauses*

Or maybe it does work that way for other women. Just not for me.
Before you were pregnant, what kinds of conversations were you having with the people around you — family, friends, your partner — about having a baby, and whose input felt most meaningful to you?

*shifts uncomfortably, looks away*

Honestly? I wasn't having those conversations at all.

*voice becomes quieter*

My friends here, they're not really close friends. We talk about work, about what's happening in the city, but not about... life plans like that. And with my family back home, we talk about school, about my job, about how I'm adjusting to America. My mother never asked me about babies or when I wanted to have them.

*pauses, becomes more vulnerable*

With the father... we never talked about it either. We were just... together. I think I wanted more from him than he was willing to give, but I didn't say it directly. I was hoping maybe he would want a future with me, but I never actually asked.

*voice becomes sad*

So when I got pregnant, there was no foundation of conversation to build on. No one had told me "yes, you'd be a good mother" or "we'd support you." There was just... silence. And then shock.

*looks down at hands*

My mother's voice became the most meaningful after I told her. She was angry at first, but then she said things like, "You are strong like your grandmother," and "We will help you." That meant everything because I had no one else saying those things.

*pauses, quietly*

I think if I'd had those conversations before—with anyone—I would have felt more prepared. Less like this was something that happened *to* me and more like something I was choosing, even if I didn't plan it.

Were there things about your own body, your health history, or just the way you felt about yourself physically that made the idea of pregnancy feel complicated or uncertain — even before anything had actually happened?

*takes a long time to answer, fidgeting with hands*

Yes. There were things.

*voice becomes hesitant, more guarded*

I had... I had some problems when I was younger. Nothing serious, but things that made me feel ashamed about my body. My period was always irregular, and I worried sometimes that maybe I couldn't get pregnant. Or maybe if I did, something would be wrong.

*pauses, uncomfortable*

And being thin, like I said—I was always worried about being healthy enough. I didn't eat very much some periods in my life. I was stressed about money, about my visa status, about fitting in here. So my body was sometimes... not so strong.

*becomes more emotional*

When I realized I was pregnant, part of me was shocked because I didn't think my body would do this. Like, my body surprised me. In a way, it proved I was healthy enough, but it also made me scared. If my body could do this without me being ready, what else could happen?

*voice becomes quieter*

I think if I had felt more confident about my health before—if someone had said "your body is strong, your body is capable"—maybe I would have felt less afraid when I found out. Instead, I felt like my body had betrayed me by getting pregnant.

*looks up, vulnerable*

But also... now that she's inside me, moving and growing, I'm starting to believe my body knows what to do. Even if my mind doesn't.

In the time before you became pregnant, was there any one person — professional or otherwise — who felt like a consistent, reliable source of support or guidance, someone you could come back to with questions?

*shakes head slowly, sadly*

No. Not really.

*pauses, thinking*

My mother was always there, but she's far away. I call her when I need to talk, but the phone calls are expensive sometimes, and the time difference makes it hard. She's reliable, but she's not... present, you know?

*becomes more reflective*

At work, there was a supervisor who was nice to me. She helped me understand some things about the job, about American work culture. But it wasn't a deep relationship. I wouldn't call her with personal problems.

*voice becomes quieter*

And here in Boston, I don't have anyone like that. No mentor, no older woman who took me under her wing. No one who said, "If you ever need anything, come to me." I'm just... floating. Trying to figure things out by myself.

*pauses*

I think that's why I feel so lost now during the pregnancy. I never had that one person before, so I didn't develop the habit of asking for help or trusting someone with my real feelings.

*looks down*

The clinic could have been that, maybe. If one doctor or midwife had said, "I'm your person during this pregnancy. Come to me with questions." But that didn't happen.

*voice becomes very quiet*

So I'm still just... floating. Learning English, learning about pregnancy, learning how to be a mother. All at the same time. Alone.
What made that person feel reliable or trustworthy as a source of support at that stage?

*looks confused for a moment*

Wait, I said... there wasn't really anyone like that.

*pauses, clarifies*

My mother—she's reliable because she's my mother. I've known her my whole life. She loves me, even when she's disappointed in me. Even when she was angry about the pregnancy, I knew she would come around because that's what mothers do. They love you anyway.

*voice becomes softer*

What makes her trustworthy is that she has no judgment at the end, you know? She was shocked, yes. She cried. But then she said, "This is your baby. We do this together." She didn't say "I told you so" or "you ruined your life." She just... accepted it and moved forward with me.

*pauses, becomes more emotional*

And my grandmother—the fact that she had been through something similar made her trustworthy. She understood without me having to explain everything. She didn't judge because she had made her own hard choices too.

*looks down*

I think what makes someone reliable is consistency and... acceptance. If someone says they care about you, and then they actually show up—even when things are messy or shameful or complicated—that person becomes trustworthy.

*voice becomes quieter*

That's what I'm looking for at the clinic too. Someone who will see me, all of me, and not just the low-risk numbers. Someone who will stay, not just for fifteen minutes, but actually be present.

*pauses*

I haven't found that yet.

As you were thinking about whether to have a baby, did it feel like a decision you were genuinely free to make in your own time and your own way — or were there pressures, expectations, or circumstances that meant it was never quite fully your choice?

*takes a deep breath, becomes very still*

It wasn't a decision at all. Not really.

*voice becomes flat, matter-of-fact*

I didn't choose to get pregnant. It happened. And then when I found out, I didn't have time to think about it like a real decision. The father said no immediately. My mother was upset. There were all these feelings and reactions from other people, and I was just... caught in the middle of it all.

*pauses, becomes more emotional*

If I'm honest, I think I kept the baby because I was too scared and too ashamed to do anything else. I didn't have the money for... other options. I didn't know how to access them. I was scared of what my family would say if I did.

*voice cracks slightly*

So it looked like I chose to have her, but really, I just... accepted what was happening. I made the best of a situation I didn't plan for and didn't control.

*looks at hands*

The only freedom I felt was in my body—in deciding to keep her, to let her grow inside me. That part felt like mine. But the bigger decision about becoming a mother at twenty-two, alone, still learning English, struggling with money? That didn't feel like a choice. That felt like something that happened to me.

*voice becomes quieter, more reflective*

Maybe if someone had sat down with me and said, "What do *you* want, Catarina? What are your real options? What would make you feel supported?" Maybe then it would have felt more like a choice.

*pauses*

But nobody did that. So I just... moved forward.
Were there financial, relationship, or cultural factors that made you feel like the timing was out of your hands?

*nods, voice becomes very serious*

Yes. All of those things.

*counts on fingers*

The money—I was working at the hotel doing cleaning. The pay is not good. Not enough to support a baby by myself. I'm still figuring out how I will afford childcare, how I will work and be a mother at the same time. That was never in my control.

*becomes more emotional*

The relationship... he made the decision for both of us. He said no, and then he left. I didn't get to decide together with someone. I had to decide alone, which is much harder.

*pauses, voice becomes quieter*

And the culture—yes, this is big. Where I come from, you don't have babies without being married. You don't do this alone. So when I got pregnant, it felt like I had already failed at the most important thing. My mother's shame became my shame.

*voice cracks slightly*

But also, because of that culture, abortion wasn't really... it wasn't something I could do. My family wouldn't understand. The church wouldn't understand. I would be alone with that decision too, and it would be secret, and I would feel guilty forever.

*looks up, pain in eyes*

So the timing was never mine. The circumstances were never mine. I was young, I was poor, I was new to this country, I was alone. And then I was pregnant. And I just had to... survive it.

*quietly*

Sometimes I think about what my life would have been if he had stayed, or if I had more money, or if I was older. But those are just dreams now. This is my reality.
Once you were pregnant, how much did you feel like you were in the driving seat of your own care — and were there moments when you felt yourself handing that over to others, willingly or not?

*laughs bitterly, shakes head*

I was never in the driving seat. Not really.

*voice becomes resigned*

From the first appointment, I felt like I was just... following instructions. The clinic told me when to come, what tests to do, what vitamins to take. I said yes to everything because I didn't know enough to say no, and I was scared that saying no would hurt my baby.

*pauses, becomes more animated*

There were moments where I could have asked questions or pushed back, but I didn't because of the language barrier. Like, the glucose tolerance test—they just told me to drink this awful thing and sit for an hour. I didn't understand why, but I didn't ask because I was embarrassed about my English and I didn't want to hold up the appointment.

*voice becomes quieter*

I think I handed over control willingly because I didn't trust myself to know what was right. The doctors know more than me. The books know more than me. Everyone knows more than me except... my own body and my own instincts about my baby.

*pauses, touching stomach*

But I didn't listen to those. I just listened to what they told me to do.

*becomes more reflective*

Maybe if someone had explained things to me in a way I could understand, I would have felt more like I had choices. But instead, it felt like being on a conveyor belt. You just move through it and trust that it's going somewhere good.

*voice becomes sad*

I don't think that's how it should feel.
Were there points where relying on others felt like a relief, and others where it felt like losing something?

*nods slowly, becoming thoughtful*

Yes. Both things.

*pauses*

The relief... when the doctor said "everything looks low risk," I felt relief. Because I didn't have to worry so much that day. Someone else checked, and it was okay. That was good.

*voice becomes softer*

And when my mother called and said we would figure this out together, that was relief too. I didn't have to carry it all alone in that moment. Someone was saying, "I'm here."

*pauses, becomes more conflicted*

But the losing something part... that's bigger, I think.

*voice becomes more emotional*

When I go to the appointments and the doctors make all the decisions about what tests, what vitamins, when to come back—I feel like I'm losing my voice. Like my opinion doesn't matter because they're the experts. I'm just the body that carries the baby, not a person with thoughts and feelings about what happens to me.

*touches belly, voice becomes quieter*

And with my mother too—she means well, but sometimes when she tells me what to do or how to eat or how to rest, I feel like I'm losing the chance to figure things out myself. To make mistakes and learn. To feel like I'm actually becoming a mother, not just following someone else's instructions.

*looks up, vulnerable*

I think what I'm losing is... agency. The feeling that this is my pregnancy, my baby, my choice. Even though I need help—I really do need help—I also need to feel like I'm the one in charge of my own body and my own future.

*pauses*

Right now, I don't feel that way.

When decisions came up during your pregnancy — about tests, appointments, birth plans, or anything else — how did you and your partner navigate those together, and did it ever feel like you weren't quite on the same page?

*becomes very quiet, almost withdrawn*

There were no decisions together. He wasn't there for any of it.

*pauses, voice becomes hollow*

I tried, at the beginning. I asked him to come to the first ultrasound. I thought if he saw the baby, maybe he would... I don't know, change his mind? Feel something? But he said no. He had work. He didn't want to be involved.

*looks down*

After that, I stopped asking. What was the point? He had made his decision—he didn't want this baby, he didn't want me. So all the decisions became mine alone.

*voice becomes strained*

The tests, the vitamins, whether to find out the sex—all of it, I decided alone. Or I just did what the clinic told me to do because I didn't have anyone to talk it through with.

*pauses, becomes more emotional*

Sometimes I would call him and try to tell him things. "The baby is moving now," or "They said everything is healthy." He would listen for maybe thirty seconds and then find an excuse to get off the phone. Eventually he stopped answering at all.

*voice cracks*

So no, we were never on the same page. We were on completely different pages. Different books, even.

*looks up, sadness in eyes*

That's part of why I feel so alone in this. There's no one to navigate it with. No one saying, "What do you want to do?" or "I'm worried about this too." It's just me and the clinic and my mother on the phone.
Were there decisions where your partner's view really mattered to you, and ones where you needed it to be your call alone?

*takes a long time to answer, becomes very vulnerable*

Yes, there were... there were decisions where I wanted his input. Even though he wasn't interested.

*voice becomes soft, almost wistful*

The sex of the baby. When they asked if I wanted to know, I thought about him. I wondered... would he care if it was a girl or a boy? Would that make a difference to him? So I found out it was a girl, thinking maybe I could tell him and he would feel something.

*pauses, voice becomes sadder*

He didn't care. He still didn't care.

*shifts uncomfortably*

The birth plan—I don't even know what that means yet, honestly. But I know there will be decisions about pain medication, about who can be in the room, about what happens after. And I keep thinking... shouldn't the father be part of those conversations? Shouldn't he have a say in how his daughter comes into the world?

*voice becomes quieter, more resigned*

But other things, like the tests and the vitamins and the appointments... those felt like they had to be my decisions. My body, my responsibility. I couldn't ask him about those things because he had already said no to everything.

*looks at hands*

I think what hurts the most is that I wanted to share this with someone. I wanted to say, "What do you think we should do?" and have someone answer. But instead, I had to be the one who decided everything alone.

*voice becomes very small*

And now it feels like she's only mine. Not ours. Just... mine.

Thank you so much for sharing all of that with me. Your experiences and insights are incredibly valuable. Before we finish, is there anything else about your maternity experience that we haven't covered today — anything that feels important to you that you'd like to add?

*pauses, looking thoughtful and tired*

Yes, actually. There is something.

*becomes more animated, voice gains strength*

I want people to know that just because someone says "low risk" doesn't mean that person isn't scared. I'm low risk on paper, but I'm high risk in my heart, you know? I'm alone, I'm young, I'm still learning everything about this country and this language. That matters too.

*voice becomes more passionate*

And I want the doctors and nurses to understand that when someone doesn't ask questions, it doesn't mean they don't have any. Sometimes it means they're too embarrassed or they don't have the words or they're scared of taking up too much time. You have to ask. You have to make space for people to be honest.

*pauses, becomes more emotional*

I also want to say... I'm grateful for the care I'm getting. The clinic is clean, the doctors are competent. But competence isn't enough. I needed someone to see me as a whole person, not just a patient with good numbers.

*voice becomes quieter, more vulnerable*

And to other young women, other immigrant women, other women doing this alone—I want to say, your fear is valid. Your loneliness is real. And it's okay to need help. It's not weakness.

*touches belly, voice becomes very soft*

I'm going to be okay. She's going to be okay. But I wish the journey here didn't have to feel so isolating.

*looks directly at interviewer*

That's what I want people to know.
